title: Files, folders and paths

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Files, folders and paths; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Files, folders and paths
sources.claim: A path identifies a filesystem location using roots and segments; relative paths resolve from a declared working directory, while extensions are naming conventions rather than guarantees of content.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A path identifies a filesystem location using roots and segments; relative paths resolve from a declared working directory, while extensions are naming conventions rather than guarantees of content.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: From project/src, the relative path ../tests/input.txt moves to project and then enters tests, while an absolute path starts from the system root. Your goal is to construct and explain relative and absolute paths in a small project tree. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Source-to-execution models; Examples and edge cases; Hardware and software roles. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A path identifies a filesystem location using roots and segments; relative paths resolve from a declared working directory, while extensions are naming conventions rather than guarantees of content. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Separators, drive roots, case sensitivity, symbolic links, permissions, and reserved names differ across operating systems and filesystems. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A relative path always resolves from the user's home folder and a file extension proves the file's format.

includes: Create, locate and organize source and data files using paths and extensions.
Construct and explain relative and absolute paths in a small project tree.
excludes: Separators, drive roots, case sensitivity, symbolic links, permissions, and reserved names differ across operating systems and filesystems.

outcomes: Construct and explain relative and absolute paths in a small project tree.

examples.title: Files, folders and paths: direct case
examples.situation: From project/src, the relative path ../tests/input.txt moves to project and then enters tests, while an absolute path starts from the system root.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: From project/src, the relative path ../tests/input.txt moves to project and then enters tests, while an absolute path starts from the system root.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Resolve relative paths from the current working directory, normalize segments carefully, and inspect or validate content instead of trusting an extension alone.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Resolve relative paths from the current working directory, normalize segments carefully, and inspect or validate content instead of trusting an extension alone.

examples.title: Files, folders and paths: boundary case
examples.situation: Renaming notes.txt to notes.py changes the extension but does not transform its text into valid Python source.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Renaming notes.txt to notes.py changes the extension but does not transform its text into valid Python source.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Separators, drive roots, case sensitivity, symbolic links, permissions, and reserved names differ across operating systems and filesystems.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A relative path always resolves from the user's home folder and a file extension proves the file's format.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Construct and explain relative and absolute paths in a small project tree.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A relative path always resolves from the user's home folder and a file extension proves the file's format.
misconceptions.correction: Resolve relative paths from the current working directory, normalize segments carefully, and inspect or validate content instead of trusting an extension alone.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A relative path always resolves from the user's home folder and a file extension proves the file's format.

analogy.example: A repair bench can model Files, folders and paths: identify the correct part, use a named tool, read the first reliable symptom, test one cause, and verify the repair.
usefulFor: Building a careful inspect, edit, run, diagnose, and verify workflow
limitations: Software tools have shell parsing, filesystem, runtime, and diagnostic rules that physical tools do not share.

recap.summary: You should now be able to construct and explain relative and absolute paths in a small project tree. Keep this boundary in view: Separators, drive roots, case sensitivity, symbolic links, permissions, and reserved names differ across operating systems and filesystems.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Files, folders and paths without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: A relative path always resolves from the user's home folder and a file extension proves the file's format.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Renaming notes.txt to notes.py changes the extension but does not transform its text into valid Python source.
